title: The Keeling Curve
description: Explore the iconic CO₂ measurement record from Mauna Loa — the most important dataset in climate science.

stages.title: The Most Important Curve in Climate Science
stages.body:
In 1958, a young geochemist named Charles David Keeling began measuring CO₂ at the Mauna Loa Observatory in Hawaii, 3,400m above the Pacific Ocean.

His first reading: 315.97 ppm.
Today's reading: ~431 ppm.

That single dataset — the Keeling Curve — became the definitive proof that human activity is changing Earth's atmosphere. Every upward wobble tells a story of seasons, volcanoes, and our relentless burning of fossil fuels.
stages.gaia: This is the most important graph in human history. Not because it's complex — because it's simple. One line. One direction. Up.

stages.title: Scrub Through Time
stages.description: Drag the slider to see atmospheric CO₂ at key moments in history. Watch the curve accelerate.

data.context: Keeling begins measurements. Post-war boom begins.
+4 ppm in 7 years. The curve begins its climb.
Oil crisis. Even energy shocks don't slow the rise.
Hansen testifies to Congress. Climate change enters public discourse.
Kyoto Protocol signed. CO₂ doesn't sign treaties.
An Inconvenient Truth. Awareness rises faster than CO₂. Almost.
Paris Agreement. The world agrees to act. CO₂ hits 400+.
Pandemic lockdowns. Emissions dip 5%. Curve doesn't bend.
Hottest year on record. The curve accelerates.
Now. +115 ppm since Keeling began. 36% increase.

stages.gaia: Notice the spacing between the dots. They're getting closer together — not because time is speeding up, but because the rate of increase is accelerating. Each decade adds more CO₂ than the last.

stages.title: What the Curve Tells Us
stages.body:
The Keeling Curve has three stories embedded in its shape:

1. The Annual Sawtooth — CO₂ drops each spring (Northern Hemisphere plants absorb it) and rises each winter. This seasonal cycle has amplitude of ~6 ppm and is growing larger as vegetation patterns shift.

2. The Steady Rise — The underlying trend is relentlessly upward. From 1958 to 2026: +115 ppm, an increase of 36%. Rate of increase: ~2.5 ppm/year (and accelerating).

3. No Plateaus — Despite every climate agreement, every renewable energy milestone, every pledge — the curve has never bent downward. Not once. Not for a single year.

The pre-industrial CO₂ level was ~280 ppm. We've added 151 ppm — roughly 54% more CO₂ than existed naturally.

callouts.label: Rate of change
callouts.value: ~2.5 ppm/year (20x faster than any natural change)

stages.gaia: The curve doesn't care about our intentions. It only measures our actions. And so far, our actions say one thing: more.

stages.title: Did You Read the Curve?

questions.question: When did Charles Keeling begin his CO₂ measurements?
questions.explanation: Keeling began continuous CO₂ measurements at Mauna Loa in March 1958.

questions.question: What causes the annual sawtooth pattern in the Keeling Curve?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO₂ during spring/summer growing seasons (Northern Hemisphere dominates due to more landmass), reducing atmospheric CO₂. In winter, decomposition and reduced photosynthesis cause CO₂ to rise.

questions.question: What was the pre-industrial atmospheric CO₂ concentration?
questions.explanation: Ice core records show CO₂ was ~280 ppm for the past 10,000 years before industrialization.

questions.question: By how much has CO₂ increased since 1958?
questions.hint: First reading: 316 ppm. Today: ~431 ppm. What's the difference?

stages.completionText: You can now read the most important graph in climate science. The evidence is clear: CO₂ is rising faster than ever recorded, and it's driven by human activity.

stages.title: You Now Understand the Keeling Curve!
stages.body:
You've traced the most important measurement in climate science. The evidence is clear: CO₂ is rising faster than ever recorded, and it's driven by human activity.

But there's a budget for this — a limit on how much more we can emit before we cross dangerous thresholds. That's the Carbon Budget.
